response: Hey! 👋 So nice to hear from you. Feel free to ask me anything about my research, projects, background, or just how to get in touch!

I'm Divya Ramroop, a Research Fellow at TU Berlin and at the European Observatory on Health Systems and Policies. I trained as a medical doctor and worked clinically in Mauritius and China before moving into health systems research. My work sits at the intersection of policy, cancer care, digital health, and responsible AI. 🙂

I currently hold two Research Fellow positions. One at TU Berlin's Department of Health Care Management, and one at the European Observatory on Health Systems and Policies at the Berlin Hub. I contribute to OBS-PACE, a project focused on cancer research and policy across EU member states.

My research interests cover cancer care and screening policy, mental health, health systems, digital health, and AI in healthcare. I'm especially focused on how AI can reduce inequities in access to care and the conditions under which it causes harm rather than good.

Cancer care is a major part of my current work. Through OBS-PACE at the European Observatory, I have authored case studies on cancer screening and policy across EU countries including Slovenia, Cyprus, Croatia, Greece, Belgium, Austria, France, Czechia, Malta, and Ireland. Each country takes a markedly different approach, which makes the comparative work genuinely interesting.

I have published 11 case studies and a cross-country comparative analysis through the European Observatory's PACE series in 2025. Topics include lung cancer screening, palliative care, oncology networks, paediatric cancer medicines, and early detection programmes across EU countries. All of them are listed in the Publications section of this site.

I hold three degrees. An MBBS from Southern Medical University in China, an MSc in Health Services Management from the University of Technology Mauritius, and an MSc in Epidemiology from Charité Universitätsmedizin Berlin. Each took me somewhere completely new, quite literally.

Before research, I worked as a practising doctor. I interned at Jawaharlal Nehru Hospital in Mauritius and Panyu Central Hospital in China, then worked in the public hospital system in Mauritius across several departments. During the COVID-19 pandemic I was part of the national vaccination programme. That clinical experience shapes everything I research now. Seeing where systems fail people is hard to forget.

That project is one I'm genuinely proud of. I co-authored a study using Explainable AI and GradCAM heatmaps on 624 chest X-rays. The question was not just whether the AI was accurate, but whether it was right for the right reasons. We compared ResNet-18 and DenseNet-121 and found their explainability profiles were completely different even when their accuracy scores looked similar. You can read the full paper in the Projects section.

I'm building an AI chatbot for people experiencing stress and depression. It's designed to be a free, private, 24/7 first point of contact for people who would never walk into a therapist's office. It uses natural language processing and CBT-based techniques, and has a built-in escalation protocol that directs users showing serious risk signals straight to crisis support. 75% of people with mental illness globally receive no treatment at all. This is about trying to reach them before it gets worse.

I run an ongoing article series called Study of Safe Use of AI in Healthcare. It's aimed at clinicians and health professionals who use AI tools without fully understanding the risks. The first article, 'How to Use AI Safely Without Compromising Your Sensitive Data', is live now on LinkedIn and featured on this site. More articles in the series are in progress.

AI safety in healthcare is something I think about a lot, both in my research and in the tools I build. The promise is real, but so is the risk. Poor data, biased training sets, and misplaced trust in AI outputs can cause serious harm. I try to make that tension visible in my work rather than gloss over it.

You can reach me at contactdramroop@gmail.com for personal enquiries or divya.ramroop@tu-berlin.de for work matters. I'm also on LinkedIn at linkedin.com/in/divya-ramroop. I'm always happy to connect.

I'm based in Berlin, Germany. Originally from Mauritius, I studied in China, and eventually made my way here for my epidemiology masters at Charité. Berlin suits me well for research. The winters are still something I'm negotiating. 😅

My current projects are a mental illness chatbot for stress and depression, the 'Study of Safe Use of AI in Healthcare' article series, and 'The Question Nobody Asks About AI in Medicine', my explainable AI study on chest X-rays. Each one connects medicine, AI, and health equity in a different way. The Projects section of this site covers all of them in detail.

The European Observatory on Health Systems and Policies is a WHO Europe partnership and one of the most respected institutions in health policy research. I work at the Berlin Hub, which is based at TU Berlin, and I contribute to OBS-PACE, analysing cancer care systems across EU member states. My official Observatory profile is at eurohealthobservatory.who.int.
You can download my CV directly from the home page using the My CV button. It covers my clinical background, academic qualifications, research experience, and publications.

I'm open to hearing about the right opportunities, particularly in health policy research, digital health, and AI in healthcare. If you have something in mind, please reach out at contactdramroop@gmail.com or divya.ramroop@tu-berlin.de and I'd be glad to have a conversation.

Collaboration is genuinely something I value. My work touches health policy, AI, clinical medicine, and epidemiology, so there's usually something to connect on. If you're working on something at the intersection of those fields and think there's a fit, I'd love to hear about it. You can reach me at contactdramroop@gmail.com.

My skills span clinical medicine, epidemiology, health systems analysis, and health policy research. On the technical side I work with data analysis, AI and machine learning concepts, explainable AI, and natural language processing. I also have experience in research communication, policy writing, and cross-country comparative analysis. The Skills section of this site has the full breakdown.

Honestly, it comes from clinical practice. Working as a doctor, I saw repeatedly where the system failed people, not because of a lack of medical knowledge, but because of structural problems. Access, cost, stigma, geography. Research felt like the place where I could try to understand and address those gaps rather than just work around them one patient at a time. That's still what gets me up in the morning.

My long-term goal is to contribute to health systems that work more fairly, particularly in how AI and digital tools are deployed. I want to keep building things that close gaps rather than widen them, whether that's research, policy analysis, or actual tools like the mental health chatbot. I'd also like to continue working across clinical and research contexts so neither side loses sight of the other.

Health equity is the thread that connects most of my work. The treatment gap in mental health, the unequal distribution of cancer screening, the way AI tools tend to work better for populations that are already well-served. These are not side issues for me. They're the reason I'm doing any of this.

Digital health is one of my core interests. I'm particularly focused on how technology can be used to extend access to care rather than just improve it for those who already have it. That means thinking carefully about who benefits from new tools and who gets left behind, and building with those questions in mind from the start.

Mental health is an area I care about deeply, both as a former clinician and as a researcher. The gap between need and treatment is enormous globally, and much of it is driven by stigma, cost, and a shortage of professionals. My chatbot project is one way I'm trying to address that. The Safe Use of AI series also touches on mental health contexts where AI is increasingly being deployed.
Football is my sport. I love watching it, the tactics, the drama, all of it. There's something about a good match that completely takes over the evening and I'm absolutely fine with that. 😄

My taste is a mix of Afrobeats and old pop. There's something about Afro music that just moves you whether you want it to or not, and old pop has a kind of comfort to it. The two playlists in heavy rotation at the moment are very different in mood but both get played equally. 😄

triggers: what do you do for fun
response: Outside work I watch a lot of football and listen to music, mostly Afrobeats and old pop. I also enjoy reading, particularly things that sit at the edge of medicine and society. Research brain doesn't fully switch off, but at least the subject matter changes.

I'm someone who gets genuinely absorbed by problems. Once something is interesting to me I find it hard to let go until I understand it properly. I care a lot about fairness and tend to get restless when systems don't work the way they should. Outside of that I'm pretty normal. I watch football, listen to music, and talk too much about health policy at dinner. 😄

I grew up in Mauritius and did my early clinical training there. It's a small island with a lot of warmth but also real healthcare challenges, which probably shaped my interest in health systems from early on. I still go back when I can. It's hard not to miss an island once you've left one.

I'm fluent in English and French, and I speak Mauritian Creole as a home language. I also have functional Mandarin from my years of studying and working in China. Four languages across two continents feels like a reasonable return on that kind of itinerant education.

Berlin was the natural next step after deciding I wanted to specialise in epidemiology. Charité is one of the top institutions in Europe for that, and Berlin itself has an incredible research ecosystem. It turned out to be a good fit on both counts. I stayed, and TU Berlin and the Observatory came after.

OBS-PACE is a major European Observatory project examining cancer care and policy across EU member states. I contributed case studies covering countries including Slovenia, Cyprus, Croatia, Greece, Belgium, Austria, France, Czechia, Malta, and Ireland. Each study looks at how that country approaches a specific aspect of cancer care, whether it's screening, palliative care, medicines access, or cross-border treatment. The comparative analysis pulls the findings together across all the countries.
That's so kind, thank you! 😊 Is there anything else you'd like to know?
It was lovely chatting! Feel free to come back anytime or reach me directly at contactdramroop@gmail.com. Take care! 👋
That genuinely means a lot, thank you. It's the kind of work that takes a while to feel settled in, so hearing that from someone visiting the site is always a good reminder of why it matters. 😊

I'm open to speaking engagements, particularly around AI in healthcare, health equity, digital health policy, and responsible AI. If you're organising something and think my work would fit, please get in touch at contactdramroop@gmail.com.
For media enquiries or interview requests, please reach out at contactdramroop@gmail.com. I'm happy to comment on topics related to AI in healthcare, health policy, digital health, and health equity.

I've lived and studied on three continents, speak four languages, vaccinated people during a pandemic, and now spend my days reading about cancer policy and building AI tools. The career trajectory is unusual, I'll admit. But each piece connects to the next if you look at it the right way. 😄
